metadata.title: Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management

abstract: The aim of this research is to introduce innovative automated text mining process to extract operation risks from accounting narratives and to further examine the association between these risk types and operating performance. Specifically, we perform topic modeling to decompose a large amount of unstructured textual disclosures into some topics and preserve these topics, which are relevant to business operation risk. Sequentially, we propose a measure for the degree of financial default, referred to as the "intensity of risk-word list," by joint utilization of text mining and a statistical approach. The analyzed results are then fed into a support vector machine-based model to construct the forecasting model. The results show that the textual-based risk indicators are significantly and positively related to a corporate's operation efficiency. This study also echoes the recent trend of financial reporting regulations to add a new section on risk factors in annual reports.

sections.paper_id: Hsu et al. - 2022 - Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management
paragraphs: Corporate risk management is essential for maintaining and protecting business operations under anticipated risk exposure. Risk aggregation, which is designed to protect a corporate against potential financial shocks and losses from different types of risks, is an attractive topic related to corporate risk management, especially in today's highly competitive atmosphere (Back et al. 2001). A lack of sufficient and useful data is one of the critical challenges of corporate risk aggregation that can result in an unreliable and invalid risk aggregation outcome (Wei et al. 2019). Researchers have found that risk data gathered from financial documents (i.e., periodical reports, earnings announcements, conference calls, etc.), which are information offered to outsiders who are not privy to insider information, can be used to solve the problem of data shortages (Xu et al. 2018). Market participants can gain from studies on these warning signals of corporate risk, which can help prevent them from encountering financial troubles and avoid chasing poor investment targets or unwise investments. Accordingly, a substantial and growing amount of studies has examined and compared the characteristics of financial documents for corporates worldwide, particularly numerical information (as the metrics of numerical information are intuitive and easy to realize), such as Altman (1968) who applied five financial ratios to construct a bankruptcy forecasting model, and Ohlson (1980) who incorporated financial ratios with discriminant analysis for financial distress prediction.

Due to complexity in gathering full narrative documents and complexity in quantifying narrative information, very little is known about the associated textual information (Lang and Stice-Lawrence 2015;Jung and Suh 2019;Zhang et al. 2021a, b). Lo et al. (2017) indicated that narrative information accounts for 80% of annual reports, and the remainder (20%) of annual reports entails numbers, tables, and figures. Relying on minority information (numerical information only) in annual reports can be questionable and problematic. Furthermore, numerical data only represent past and current performances, but do not reflect any accurate future forecast. To combat this, narrative information that complements the problem of information hysteresis by considering numerical information only should be taken into account so as to depict the underlying reality of business operations (Malesios et al. 2020;Izadikhah and Farzipoor Saen 2020;Roy et al. 2020).

Compared to professional institutes and financial analysts, a large proportion of market participants lacks sufficient knowledge and experience and cannot tell which information is relevant to their needs. In an unfamiliar domain, market participants tend to gather as much information as possible. Unfortunately, too much information might confuse and mislead their decision-making process. Thus, how to decompose a large amount of narrative information into small subsets and preserve the relevant subsets for further analysis turns out to be a beneficial task to attain.

Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) is a generative probabilistic model that has demonstrated its usefulness in topic extraction (Blei 2012). Because topic determination by this method does not involve humans, an unbiased and objective outcome can be reached (Graneheim and Lundman 2004;Mimno et al. 2011;Lee and Sohn 2019;Zhang et al. 2021a, b). LDA also preserves the topics that are relevant to business operation risks (i.e., those topics not relevant to business operation risks are filtered), examines their impact on business operations, and provides some appropriate strategies in response to these risks. Another essential issue is how to convert preserved topics that are full of textual information into numerical indicators. By joint utilization of text mining (TM) and a statistical approach, an "intensity of risk-word list (IRWL)" can be established. After counting the frequency of each word in IRWL shown in each preserved topic, we are able to convert textual-based information into numerical-based information, which is the most intuitive format for users to realize/calculate and to shape managers' attitude toward corporate risks.

In contrast to financial crisis and credit risk prediction, which have been broadly examined, research on operating performance forecasting is quite scant. Undoubtedly, the nature of performance evaluation is a multi-dimensional task, and past studies concentrate mainly on a one-input and one-output architecture (such as return on assets (ROA): net income/total assets; return on equity (ROE): net income/total equity), which do not represent the reality of corporate operations very well (Seiford and Zhu 1999;Basso et al. 2018). To combat this, data envelopment analysis (DEA) can be conducted, as it handles multiple inputs and outputs simultaneously without a pre-determined production function. Traditional DEA establishes an efficient frontier among all decision-making units (DMUs), by relying on a calculation procedure that assesses the ratio scales of the weighted sum of the outputs to that of the inputs (López and Maldonado 2017). The DMUs on the efficient frontier are deemed as efficient, and the DMUs not on the efficient frontier are deemed as inefficient. Cooper et al. (2000) indicated that the set of optimal weights for the efficient DMU is actually the set of most favorable weights for that DMU in the sense that it achieves the highest performance score-that is, the efficient DMU is identified via DEA in the most favorable situation. The frontier constructed by DEA can be called the best-practice frontier (BPF). Cielen et al. (2004) and Sueyoshi (2006) performed BPF-DEA to handle the task of bankruptcy prediction, but BPF-DEA identifies potentially distressed companies by measuring how inefficient they are under the most favorable situation, which is not appropriate for practical applications. In a highly fluctuating environment, potential companies may get liquidated first and are usually the ones that are the least competitive in comparison with others as the situation turns worse, especially when an economic depression arises such as the 2008 financial tsunami and the European debt crisis that followed.

The worst-practice frontier (WPF) DEA introduced by Liu and Chen (2009) can handle the aforementioned problem. No matter what types of frontiers the DEA adopts (BPF or WPF), they may also suffer poor discrimination; i.e., more than one DMU may be categorized as efficient, thus preventing a full ranking of these units (Oukil and Amin 2015). To achieve better discrimination, the theory of multiple attribute decision-making (MADM) can be considered (Abdel-Basset et al. 2020). The technique for order preference by similarity to ideal solution (TOPSIS) (Hwang and Yoon 1981) is one type of MADM approach that aims at selecting an alternative that is the shortest distance from the positive ideal solution and the longest distance from the negative ideal solution. TOPSIS can be incorporated into DEA to reach the goal of full ranking (i.e., performance evaluation) and can further examine the association between operating performance and those risk types derived from accounting narratives (Gan and Lee 2019;Pelissari et al. 2020;Berenguer et al. 2020).

The analyzed outcomes are then injected into a support vector machine (SVM)based algorithm, named the projection twin support vector machine (PTWSVM), to construct the forecasting model. This algorithm not only has an equivalent generalization ability like SVM, but also takes up less computational efforts than SVM (i.e., its computational speed is around four times faster) (Jayadeva et al. 2007). One of the critical challenges of SVM-based algorithms (i.e., PTWSVM) is a lack of comprehensibility-that is, the inherent decision logics are opaque. If users cannot examine or test the decision logics derived from PTWSVM, then they will decline to accept this model, thus impeding its practical applications. To combat this, fish swarm algorithm (FSA) (Li et al. 2002) opens up the black-box and represents the decision logics in a human-readable format. This study introduces a trustworthy forecasting model, examines the association between risk types derived from accounting narratives and operating performance, and also provides guidance for users to realize continuous improvement as well as increases its competitive advantages so as to reach the goal of sustainable development.

Our research offers considerable implications for decision makers, market participants, and policy-makers. First, our work introduces an innovative decision architecture that integrates LDA and TM in order to extract the inherent semantics of accounting narratives. By performing LDA, we are able to decompose a large amount of textual information in accounting narratives into specific topics and preserve those topics relevant to business operation risks. IRWL is established via TM and a statistical approach. By counting the frequency of each word in IRWL shown in each preserved topics, we are able to translate a large amount of textual information into numerical indicators and further examine the association between these risk types and operating performance. Even a little bit improvement in decisionmaking quality can bring a considerable amount of future savings.

TOPSIS-DEA, which integrates BPF-DEA and WPF-DEA, helps determine a corporate's operating performance. Based on our findings, decision makers can now devote more time to these specific topics and modify their operating strategies with regard to relevant issues. Through this model, market participants are able to reduce the effort spent on less relevant items so as to prevent information overload. The decision logics derived from the introduced architecture help cut down on uncertainty and gain a better understanding of the consequences of publicizing specific accounting narratives. Finally, we make suggestions for policy-makers on how to align the current structure of regulatory filings with our observation of actual reporting needs.

The reminder of this study is structured as follows. Section 2 reviews the literature. Section 3 describes the applied methodologies. Section 4 expresses the empirical examinations. Section 5 offers conclusions and future works.

sections.paper_id: Hsu et al. - 2022 - Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management
paragraphs: Financial difficulties/crises have recently and more commonly been sweeping across the major economies of the world, threatening the stability of global stock markets as well as disrupting the smooth functioning of capital markets (Sun et al. 2019). To confront such problems, one urgent requirement is to construct a reliable and trustworthy pre-warning model that can be like an alarm beforehand. In the early strand of financial difficulty/crisis prediction literature, Beaver (1966) introduced the univariate discriminant model, Altman (1968) extended it to a multivariate discriminant model, and Ohlson (1980) adopted the logistic regression model. These models are statistical-based and have the advantage of a simple structure, less parameters, and less training time, but still come with some challenges, such as normal distribution and equal covariance among independent variables and zero multi-collinearity (Sun et al. 2020).

With the great improvement of information technology and the Internet, researchers have gradually switched their focuses from statistical-based models to artificial intelligence (AI)-based models, because these models have superior computing capability and do not need to obey strict statistical assumptions (Jakšič and Marinč 2019). Frydman et al. (1985) set up decision tree (DT) to construct a forecasting model for financial crisis, Odom and Sharda (1990) built a neural network (NN) to establish a financial crisis forecasting model, and Kim and Sohn (2010) used support vector machine (SVM) to handle the same task. However, in contrast to widely examined studies on financial crisis and credit risk forecasting, works on performance forecasting are quite scarce. Kamei (1997) pointed out that bad operating performance accounts for 99% of all financial crises. Thus, a prewarning model for performance forecasting is an essential and important topic to investigate.

Because numerical information is easy to access and understand, it can be useful for constructing a pre-warning model. However, numerical information and data comprise only around 20% of all information contained in firms' annual reports (Beattie et al. 2004). Thus, it is beneficial to realize how financial information and non-financial information (i.e., textual information) in annual reports are connected and how non-financial information could be used to complement the problem of information hysteresis (when only financial information is considered). This statement is echoed by Gajzler (2010) who indicated that the reason existing forecasting models have no ability to completely explain the relationships between past and future performances is due to the omission of textual information that can be extracted from annual reports.

Predictive models incorporating textual information confront some challenges to the modeling process due to several reasons. First, the textual data are natural language: they cannot be directly quantified as input in many mathematical measures. Second, the textual databases are normally much greater in size compared to numerical measures. Thus, how to quantify and point out the most relevant pieces of textual information turns out to be a crucial step. With the recent advances in statistical natural language processing (NLP), a considerable amount of literature has conducted content analysis to quantify the tone and content of descriptions so as to gain deeper insights for advanced decisions.

Content analysis can be roughly divided into two main categories. The first category is word lists, in which each word is pre-classified as positive or negative based on human judgment. Prevalent studies are the Harvard IV psychological dictionary or linguistic inquiry and word count (LIWC). Tetlock (2007) relied on the predecided word lists to realize investors' sentiment and further examine their impact on stock market movement. Magnusson et al. (2005) applied self-organizing maps to visualize the changes in writing style of annual reports in the telecommunications industry. Their results stated that when corporates are expected to perform well, their annual reports are full of optimistic and positive tones and vocabularies. Although the pre-decided word lists perform a satisfactory job in previous tasks, they also come with come challenges: a lack of objectivity and the need for large amount of human involvement. Loughran and McDonald (2001) also indicated that the Harvard word lists might not be suitable for accounting and finance studies, because many words categorized into positive or negative groups might not have such connotations in a financial context (Jegadeesh and Wu 2013). Thus, they developed a domain-specific word lists based on 10-K reports, and the results showed that their negative word list can capture the tone of 10-K reports more precisely than Harvard word lists.

The second category is how each word in the word lists is weighted, which along with its word lists enables the algorithm to convert descriptive narratives into quantitative numbers. Most previous research studies adopted a proportional weighting scheme, in which the tone is calculated by the number of positive or negative words to total number of words in a document. The assumption behind this method is that it views all words in the word lists as equally important. However, it is widely acknowledged that some words are more essential than others, and an algorithm quantifying narratives into scores should take relative word impact into consideration. Manning and Schütze (1999) introduced a weighting system that weights each word inversely proportional to document frequency. Jegadeesh and Wu (2013) objectively derived the weight of each word from market reaction. Pröllochs et al. (2018) adopted LASSO to extract decisive words. The above-mentioned weighting schemes can derive comprehensive word lists in a statistical procedure without human interventions and also be tailored to arbitrary domain-specific utilization. Grounded on this concept, the present study aims at developing an automated text mining process that infers risk-related topics from the language itself in a computerized fashion and matches them with word lists with statistical rigor so as to yield much more objective messages that help formulate a reliable decision outcome.

To confront such problems, one urgent requirement is to construct a reliable and trustworthy pre-warning model that can be like an alarm beforehand. In the early strand of financial difficulty/crisis prediction literature, Beaver (1966) introduced the univariate discriminant model, Altman (1968) extended it to a multivariate discriminant model, and Ohlson (1980) adopted the logistic regression model. These models are statistical-based and have the advantage of a simple structure, less parameters, and less training time, but still come with some challenges, such as normal distribution and equal covariance among independent variables and zero multi-collinearity (Sun et al. 2020).

With the great improvement of information technology and the Internet, researchers have gradually switched their focuses from statistical-based models to artificial intelligence (AI)-based models, because these models have superior computing capability and do not need to obey strict statistical assumptions (Jakšič and Marinč 2019). Frydman et al. (1985) set up decision tree (DT) to construct a forecasting model for financial crisis, Odom and Sharda (1990) built a neural network (NN) to establish a financial crisis forecasting model, and Kim and Sohn (2010) used support vector machine (SVM) to handle the same task. However, in contrast to widely examined studies on financial crisis and credit risk forecasting, works on performance forecasting are quite scarce. Kamei (1997) pointed out that bad operating performance accounts for 99% of all financial crises. Thus, a prewarning model for performance forecasting is an essential and important topic to investigate.

Because numerical information is easy to access and understand, it can be useful for constructing a pre-warning model. However, numerical information and data comprise only around 20% of all information contained in firms' annual reports (Beattie et al. 2004). Thus, it is beneficial to realize how financial information and non-financial information (i.e., textual information) in annual reports are connected and how non-financial information could be used to complement the problem of information hysteresis (when only financial information is considered). This statement is echoed by Gajzler (2010) who indicated that the reason existing forecasting models have no ability to completely explain the relationships between past and future performances is due to the omission of textual information that can be extracted from annual reports.

Predictive models incorporating textual information confront some challenges to the modeling process due to several reasons. First, the textual data are natural language: they cannot be directly quantified as input in many mathematical measures. Second, the textual databases are normally much greater in size compared to numerical measures. Thus, how to quantify and point out the most relevant pieces of textual information turns out to be a crucial step. With the recent advances in statistical natural language processing (NLP), a considerable amount of literature has conducted content analysis to quantify the tone and content of descriptions so as to gain deeper insights for advanced decisions.

Content analysis can be roughly divided into two main categories. The first category is word lists, in which each word is pre-classified as positive or negative based on human judgment. Prevalent studies are the Harvard IV psychological dictionary or linguistic inquiry and word count (LIWC). Tetlock (2007) relied on the predecided word lists to realize investors' sentiment and further examine their impact on stock market movement. Magnusson et al. (2005) applied self-organizing maps to visualize the changes in writing style of annual reports in the telecommunications industry. Their results stated that when corporates are expected to perform well, their annual reports are full of optimistic and positive tones and vocabularies. Although the pre-decided word lists perform a satisfactory job in previous tasks, they also come with come challenges: a lack of objectivity and the need for large amount of human involvement. Loughran and McDonald (2001) also indicated that the Harvard word lists might not be suitable for accounting and finance studies, because many words categorized into positive or negative groups might not have such connotations in a financial context (Jegadeesh and Wu 2013). Thus, they developed a domain-specific word lists based on 10-K reports, and the results showed that their negative word list can capture the tone of 10-K reports more precisely than Harvard word lists.

The second category is how each word in the word lists is weighted, which along with its word lists enables the algorithm to convert descriptive narratives into quantitative numbers. Most previous research studies adopted a proportional weighting scheme, in which the tone is calculated by the number of positive or negative words to total number of words in a document. The assumption behind this method is that it views all words in the word lists as equally important. However, it is widely acknowledged that some words are more essential than others, and an algorithm quantifying narratives into scores should take relative word impact into consideration. Manning and Schütze (1999) introduced a weighting system that weights each word inversely proportional to document frequency. Jegadeesh and Wu (2013) objectively derived the weight of each word from market reaction. Pröllochs et al. (2018) adopted LASSO to extract decisive words. The above-mentioned weighting schemes can derive comprehensive word lists in a statistical procedure without human interventions and also be tailored to arbitrary domain-specific utilization. Grounded on this concept, the present study aims at developing an automated text mining process that infers risk-related topics from the language itself in a computerized fashion and matches them with word lists with statistical rigor so as to yield much more objective messages that help formulate a reliable decision outcome.

sections.paper_id: Hsu et al. - 2022 - Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management
paragraphs: Financial reports are the main sources of corporate risk aggregation, but most prior studies merely considered numerical information to aggregate corporate risk. One of the critical challenges of numerical information is information hysteresis-that is, numerical information can be viewed as a backward-looking message that only describes historical risk profiles and does not reflect the future condition of corporate risk. Particularly, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has emphasized that market participants have a tremendous need for forwarding-looking disclosures than for disclosures about past events, and so it announced a guidance for corporates to describe any known risks and uncertainties that are likely to cause material side effects on their future business operations. In view of the essence of forward-looking messages, this study aims at analyzing the content of such information disclosed in annual report (i.e., Financial Highlights and Analysis Section) and to incorporate it with backward information to arrive at conclusive findings.

Not all of the disclosed information in forward-looking messages is relevant to business operations. To combat this, one type of topic modeling technique, called latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) (please refer to Appendix A for a more detailed illustration on it) is considered to extract hidden valuable messages. Perplexity is a probability-based measure that can evaluate how well a model will fit a sample and is widely adopted to determine the optimal number of topics for LDA (Blei et al. 2003;Wallach et al. 2009). Based on the result of perplexity and after consulting the framework of risk management standards [Committee of Sponsoring Organization: COSO (2004), and Institute of Risk Management: IRM (2002)], the preserved riskrelated topics are operational, strategic, financial, and hazardous.

sections.header_norm: intensity of risk word list irwl establishment
paragraphs: How to quantify a large amount of textual information (i.e., risk-related topics) in a timely fashion is an attractive issue for decision makers, because such information can complement the information hysteresis to numerical information without a long time delay and ameliorate the problem of information asymmetry (Back et al. 2001;Peters et al. 2016). This differs from the work of Jegadeesh and Wu (2013) who adopted a regression-based approach to determine the polarity of each word based on market reaction. The binary classification concept is conducted to infer the polarity of each word in this study. ROA is the most widely adopted ratio for understanding a corporate's financial performance (Shirata et al. 2011). Based on this assessment measure, we prioritize the data from superior to inferior and then collect the annual reports of the top 10% and bottom 10% of firms based on this measure. By doing this, we are able to categorize the data into two different distinct groupsthat is, the results appear to be derived from the classification model. We choose terms from two distinct groups exhibiting high term frequency as the representative Decision makers (policy makers, market participants, managers, and so on)

Financial reports Narrative information LDA Risk type 1 Risk type n … Numerical information Different group determination via ROA Relative weight of each term determination via Entropy Chi square Special terms for "Good performance" Special terms for "Bad performance" Intensity of risk-word list establishment (IRWL) Performance determination by TOPSIS-DEA Performance rank Initiate PTWSVM Finalized PTWSVM Reach stop criterion Update parameters Rule extraction via FSA Financial ratios Risk types Yes No Procedure 1 Procedure 3 Procedure 4 Procedure 5 Procedure 2

Fig. 1 The introduced innovative decision architecture characteristics of each group. The discriminant ability of each term can be evaluated by Chi-square ( X 2 ) (Frake et al. 1992;Yang and Pedersen 1997), which has demon- strated its usefulness in text categorization (Chen and Chen 2011) (please refer to Appendix B for a more detailed illustration on it).

To extract the different degrees of polarity among terms with statistical rigor, Entropy (please refer to Appendix B), which is widely applied in computational linguistic analysis as a measure of the relative weights among all the special terms, is considered. After going through the whole processes (Chi-square + Entropy), the "intensity of risk-word list (IRWL)" is established. By counting the frequency of each word in IRWL shown in preserved risk-related topics derived from LDA, we are able to quantify the accounting narratives into numerical information immediately without any human involvement. This benefits the speed of decision-making.

sections.paper_id: Hsu et al. - 2022 - Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management
paragraphs: Corporate risk disclosures highly relate to operating performance. DEA has demonstrated its effectiveness and widespread utilization in performance evaluation. While traditional DEA relies on the best-practice frontier (BPF) to determine the performance rank for each DMU, the concept of BPF adoption is not appropriate (Liu and Chen 2009), because firms that encounter financial troubles that result in bankruptcy are usually those with the least risk-absorbing ability among all DMUs when the situation is getting worse (more unfavorable).

We thus deem that the DEA approach based on the worst-practice frontier (WPF) is more reliable to evaluate and rank bad performance under the most unfavorable (worst-case) scenario. No matter what types of frontiers the DEA takes (BPF or WPF), they still come with the problem of poor discrimination. To handle this, one MADM technique (namely, TOPSIS) is considered by combining two different types of DEA (i.e., BPF-DEA and WPF-DEA) so as to eliminate the problem of a full ranking task (please refer to Appendix C for a more detailed illustration on it). Based on this idea, we are able to understand the reality of a firm's business operations.

paragraphs: The analyzed results (financial ratios from numerical information, risk types from textual information, and performance rank from TOPSIS-DEA) are then inserted into PTWSVM to construct the forecasting model. This study divides the whole dataset into two different data subgroups: one for training and one for testing; and five-fold cross-validation (CV) is implemented to optimize the parameters. In fivefold CV, the training data are divided into five equally sized disjoint subsets, and the training of the classifier is utilized on all the subsets with the exception of one, which is called evaluation data. The forecasting quality of PTWSVM on the testing dataset using optimal parameters is displayed as forecasting accuracy. The optimal parameters (i.e., two penalty parameters: m 1 , m 2 ) corresponding to the classifica- tion model are obtained from the range [10 -5 ~ 10 5 ] via a grid search approach. This study sets the step size to 1 and the number of iterations to 5000-that is, the optimal parameters are decided by the model with the best forecasting quality in these 5000 iterations. The Gaussian kernel ( K(x, y) = exp -(‖x-y‖ 2 ∕ 2 ) ) is considered to han- dle the non-linear cases, and the parameter is optimized from the range [2 -5 ~ 2 5 ]. To eliminate computational burdens and costs, we make m 1 = m 2 . The analysis of our model on the parameters is done in Fig. 2. A more detailed illustration on PTWSVM can be seen in Appendix D.

• Procedure 5: Rule generation PTWSVM has demonstrated its superior forecasting quality and outstanding generalization ability, but it still comes with a challenge in that the inherent decision logics are like a black-box. If the users cannot evaluate or judge the model's inherent decision logics, then they have a higher possibility to not use this model as well as impede its practical application. The methods adopted to handle the opaque nature of SVM-based model (i.e., PTWSVM) can be divided into two groups: one relies on the SVM-based model's component (such as decision surface, support vectors, and so on) for rule generation, and the other is based on rule extraction algorithms (Barakat and Bradley 2010). Barakat and Diederich (2005) also indicated that the integration of the SVM-based model and rule generation algorithm equips the black-box model with an explanation ability as well as provide end users with an intuitive and comprehensible format. The idea behind this method is to establish artificially labeled instances where the target class label of a training instance is replaced by PTWSVM's forecasted outcome. The artificial dataset is then utilized with another rule generation algorithm with explanation ability, which learns what PTWSVM has learned. The fish swarm algorithm (FSA) (Li et al. 2002), an Fig. 2 The conceptual structure of a grid search emerging population-based evolutionary computation approach inspired by the natural social behavior of fish schools and swarm intelligence and offer the ability to handle complex non-linear high-dimensional tasks with efficient convergence speed, has been widely applied to handle the optimization task and has gained much success, such as knowledge generation, (Chen et al. 2015), parameter determination (Zhang et al. 2021a, b), assembly line balancing problems (Zhong et al. 2019), and so on. Thus, this study takes FSA to extract previously unknown and valuable rules from PTWSVM and expresses the acquired knowledge in a comprehensive and intuitive manner. A more detailed illustration on FSA appears in Appendix E.

This study divides the whole dataset into two different data subgroups: one for training and one for testing; and five-fold cross-validation (CV) is implemented to optimize the parameters. paragraphs: Due to the trend among committees of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) toward a knowledge-based economy, capital-intensive industries have become the main trigger for economic growth and development. In developed economies, like the U.S. and U.K., capital-intensive industries account for over 40% of total gross domestic product (GDP). Countries with developing economies also have a similar situation. Most industries in developing economies have made a considerable effort to become manufacturers of knowledge-intensive products. For example, Taiwan's knowledge-intensive industries contribute over 30% to GDP (Directorate-General of Budget, Accounting and Statistics, Executive Yuan, 2019), and these industries have also become the world's main suppliers of integrated circuit (IC) chips, laptop computers, liquid crystal displays, and personal digital assistants (Lee et al., 2009). Among all the industries, the semiconductor industry plays the most essential role and has become the fuel for Taiwan's economic growth. This industry is characterized by long manufacturing lead time, complicated manufacturing processes, increasingly short product life, and considerable capital requirement (Wang and Chiu 2014). In fact, the electronics industry's stock trading volume exceeds 50% of the local equity market's volume turnover on average. As this industry has a great impact on the economy and dominates the local stock market, we take it as our research target.

sections.paper_id: Hsu et al. - 2022 - Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management
sections.header_raw: Risk type determination and IRWL establishment
sections.header_norm: risk type determination and irwl establishment
paragraphs: Merely considering numerical information in financial reports to form a final decision is not truly effective. This is because numerical information just represents the corporate's past and current performances and does not cover future operating potential. To combat this, narrative information in financial reports can be considered.

This study collects publicly listed IC industry firms' annual reports from the years 2015 to 2019. We focus on the section of "Financial Highlights and Analysis" in annual reports that describe the risks a corporate is confronting and how it is responding to them and its future development and investment portfolios. Table 1 displays the basic descriptive statistics of the collected documents. Next, we initialize some standard data-preprocessing processes, such as converting to lowercase, omitting stop words without any meaning, deleting contact addresses and XML/ HTML formatting, tokenizing them to terms, and performing Porter's stemming. From the domain of natural language processing (NLP), we transform the unstructured textual information into a structured style that is necessary for the following advanced analysis. The processed data are inserted into LDA to break down annual reports into various topics. The numbers of topics to be preserved are based on perplexity measure. Based on the result (see Fig. 3), we preserve four topics related to business operation risk (i.e., the perplexity value goes down dramatically after four topics are decided): (1) operational risk, (2) strategic risk, (3) financial risk, and (4) hazard risk. Table 2 shows the word lists of each risk type.

Textual information can bring a timely message without any long delay, and therefore quantifying it efficiently turns out to be an urgent requirement in today's highly dynamic business atmosphere. In response to this challenge, "IRWL (intensity of risk-word list)" is established. We use ROA to prioritize a corporate's operating performance from superior to inferior, where the top 10% of corporates are categorized in the "Good-performance group" and the bottom 10% of corporates are categorized in the "Bad-performance group." Sequentially, the Chi-square can extract the representative terms for each group-that is, the optimistic terms are for the "Good-performance group," and the pessimistic terms are for the "Bad-performance group." Finally, entropy is performed to determine the strength of each term. The weight of the term in IRWL is different, because it is computed on different data sources, such as the entire dataset, training dataset, or evaluation dataset. This study takes the training dataset into consideration to establish IRWL and further examines the impact of IRWL on the model's forecasting performance. Table 3 displays IWRL from CV-1 (that is, due to five-fold CV being implemented, we can generate five different IWRL). By counting the frequency of each word in IRWL shown in the preserved risk-related topic, we can conjecture a manager's attitude toward each risk type.

We take one of the world's largest IC packaging companies (Advanced Semiconductor Engineering: ASE) in Taiwan (stock ticker: 3711) as an example. We calculate the disclosure frequency of each term among four different types of risks to realize what type of risk attracts the highest attention. The value of each one is:

(1) operational risk at 0.24, (2) strategic risk at 0.35, (3) financial risk at 0.32, and (4) hazard risk at 0.09. (i.e., the disclosure frequency of each risk type divided by the total disclosure frequency). We know that ASE focuses more on strategic risk (i.e., because the value of strategic risk is the highest among the other three values). Sequentially, we match ASE's annual report with IRWL to quantify its managers' attitude toward dissimilar risk types. If the synthesized optimistic outcome (i.e., the frequency of each optimistic string * the weight of each optimistic string) is greater than the synthesized pessimistic outcome, then we can conjecture that corporate managers' attitude toward strategic risk is like believing they can handle it very well. For example, if this paragraph has one optimistic string (Growth: 0.035) and one pessimistic string (Risk: 0.033), each of which is disclosed three times, then the final outcome will be 0.006 (the synthesized optimistic outcome: 0.035*3 -the synthesized pessimistic outcome: 0.033*3).

paragraphs: Because the nature of performance evaluation has a multi-dimensional, traditional simple architecture (i.e., one-input and one-output variable), it cannot capture the full picture of corporate operations (Basso et al., 2008;Shafiee et al. 2014). To combat this, we consider TOPSIS-DEA as it integrates two different types of DEA (i.e., BPF-DEA and WPF-DEA). In accordance with the work done by Xu and Wang (2009), we choose total assets (TA), total liabilities (TL), and cost of goods sold (COGS) as input variables and net sales (NS) and net profit (NP) as output variables.

To make sure whether the selected input and output variables are representative or not, we perform the Pearson correlation. We can see that the selected variables show a significantly positive relationship between inputs and outputs (see Table 4)-that is, no selected variables need to be deleted. By utilizing the TOPSIS-DEA model, we are able to realize which corporate exhibits superior performance (i.e., this hybrid model can be used to handle the full ranking task). To demonstrate the effectiveness of TOPSIS-DEA in discriminant

Table 3 The result of IRWL from CV-1 Optimistic terms Pessimistic terms Term Weight Term Weight Growth 0.035 Decline 0.035 Expansion 0.034 Loss 0.034 Active 0.033 Recession 0.034 Gain 0.033 Deficit 0.033 Earnings 0.032 Risk 0.033 Net income 0.031 Net loss 0.032 Surplus 0.031 Decrease 0.032 Revenue 0.030 Suspect 0.031 Profit 0.030 Shortage 0.030 Increase 0.029 Crisis 0.029 Key factor 0.028 Decrease 0.029 Rise 0.028 Uncertainty 0.028 Demand 0.028 Fell 0.028 Lead 0.027 Descend 0.027 Improve 0.026 Default 0.027 Overweight 0.025 Weakness 0.026 Value 0.024 Violation 0.025 Potential 0.023 Ebb 0.025 ability, we consider three scenarios: (1) performance determination by BPF-DEA, (2) performance determination by WPF-DEA, and (3) TOPSIS-DEA. The results

show that TOPSIS-DEA has better discriminant ability than the other two models (i.e., it poses lower mean and higher variance) (see Table 5). It clearly shows that TOPSIS-DEA has superior power of discriminant ability as indicated by the widest score spread and the lowest mean, which spur users to do performance analysis as well as lead them to appropriate resource allocation tasks (Paradi et al. 2011). To improve the performance of an inefficient DMU, the target of improvement should be given beforehand. Both BPF-DEA and WPF-DEA generate too much guidance for inefficient DMUs to follow, which will confuse and bias them. On the contrary, TOPSIS-DEA provides one specific way for inefficient DMUs to improve their performance. Managers can really concentrate on this specific target and try to identify and imitate its strengths so as to keep up with the industry leader. Before corporates go out of business, they typically undergo a period of inefficient operations. Kamei (1997) also indicated that bad operating performance can account for 99% of financial distresses, making bad operating performance highly correlated to a financial crisis. Thus, the independent variables adopted to construct the model for financial distress prediction can be taken as surrogates to construct the model for operating performance forecasting. In line with prior related work (Chaudhuri and Bose 2020;Hsu et al. 2021;Hsu and Lin 2021), the selected financial independent variables are displayed in Table 6. Next, we calculate the frequency of each word in IRWL shown in each preserved risk-related topic. By doing that we can know each section's relevance to which type of risk. To quantify the textual information in each section, we have to match the textual information in each section with IRWL, because this derives the optimistic score and pessimistic score in each section. If the aggregation of optimistic scores minus the aggregation of pessimistic scores is positive, then this type of risk can be handled properly. If the aggregation of optimistic scores minus the aggregation of pessimistic scores is negative, then this type of risk has to be handled carefully in order to prevent fatal financial shocks from arising. We can see that a corporate with good operating performance usually has higher profitability, better assets utilization and allocation, lower financial burden, and superior cost and risk control. Most financial ratios are gathered from financial reports which may be contaminated by some degree of errors (such as accounting principle selection, estimation error, and subjective biased decision). To combat this, essential feature identification/extraction (FE) is an inevitable pre-process in data analysis (Chen et al. 2021). Principal component analysis (PCA) is a well-known data-driven approach for feature extraction, because it is able to handle high-dimensional, highly correlated, noisy data by projecting the data onto a lower-dimensional space that contains most of the variance of the original data (Navi et al. 2018). Unfortunately, this approach is linear and only can extract linear features; when it comes to handle non-linear systems, the discriminant result is unsatisfactory and improper. The kernel PCA (KPCA) (Schölkopf et al. 1997) (an extension of PCA) maps the original data into a high-dimensional feature space via kernel metric adoption and then computes PCA in the high-dimensional feature space (Cao et al. 2003).

It thus not only can extract the linear part, but also can handle the non-linear part, and so it is very suitable to handle real-life application tasks (i.e., because reallife cases are normally mixed with a linear and non-linear data structure). Thus, KPCA is conducted to identify the essential features. To realize the effectiveness of KPCA, we divide our study into two conditions: (1) feature extraction by PCA, and (2) feature extraction by KPCA (see Table 7). Five-fold CV is considered.
The results show that the model with KPCA performs better than the model with PCA.

One of the interesting findings is that the risk factors are selected by KPCA. To demonstrate the usefulness of risk factors, we consider three scenarios: (1) the model with financial information only, (2) the model with risk factors derived from textual information only, and (3) the model with financial and risk factors. To robust our finding, we conduct the five-fold CV and t-test and show the results in Table 8. We can see that the model with numerical and textual information 2016) who stated that textual information can complement the information loss from numerical information without a long time delay and yields more overarching support for decision makers to understand the reality of business operations.

To robust our research findings, this study further takes a proposed model as a benchmark and compares it with three other AI-based methods: C4.5 decision tree (DT), three-layer backpropagation neural network (BPNN), and extreme learning machine (ELM). The inherent parameters' setting for AI-based methods plays an essential role in forecasting model construction. This study considers four different scenarios (the number of hidden nodes is 8, 16, 32, and 64; and the learning epochs are 50, 100, 200, and 400) to determine the best setting for three-layer BPNN (Tsai and Wu 2008), and the other two methods are based on previous related studies (Tsai et al. 2009;Huang et al. 2006) to determine the model's inherent parameters. Five-fold cross-validation (CV) is conducted to eliminate the problem of over-fitting. The Friedman test is adopted to ensure quality of the comparison (see Table 9). We find that the introduced model performs better than the other three models under all assessment measures.

To demonstrate the effectiveness of IRWL, this study takes it as a baseline and compares it with the other three sentiment dictionaries (i.e., Harvard IV, Loughran-McDonald (2011), andPröllochs et al. (2018)). We see that the dictionary label deviates considerably from the psychological domain to the finance and accounting domain (see Table 10). One possible reason is that users normally employ implicit polarity words to express their opinions that are not covered in psychological dictionary. We note that the sentiment word list we introduce has superior discriminant ability over the other two sentiment word lists. This outcome is the same as the work done by Pröllochs et al. (2018) who indicated the weakness of a manually selected dictionary. It is because the terms classified as either positive or negative rely on human judgements that make them prone to severe drawbacks as follows. (1) The word lists decided ex ante are based on the subjective opinions of the users. (2) It lacks comprehensive and statistical rigor. 3) Too many humans involved in dictionary construction prevent it from being tailored to arbitrary domain-specific utilizations (Pröllochs et al. 2018). sections.paper_id: Hsu et al. - 2022 - Automated text mining process for corporate risk analysis and management
paragraphs: This study introduces an innovative automated text mining process (i.e., LDA + TM + TOPSIS-DEA + PTWSVM-FSA) to extract corporate risks from accounting narratives and then matches them with a domain-specific word list to translate a large amount of textual information into manageable information and numerical information that are easy to use so as to enhance managers' decisionmaking quality. Specifically, LDA breaks down textual information into some topics and preserves those topics relevant to business operation risk. In order to quantify the textual information in each risk topic, we conduct the domain-specific word list (i.e., IRWL) by performing TM and a statistical method. After matching the preserved risk topics with IRWL, we can realize which type of risk corporates are focusing more attention on and thus understand their managers' attitude toward the risk they are encountering. By extracting these linguistic cues from annual reports, users can realize more about the reality of business operations as well as prevent them from getting stuck in financial difficulties. We further examine the association between risk types and corporate operating performance. The nature of performance evaluation is multi-dimensional. It is difficult for traditional simple frameworks (i.e., ROA and ROE) to describe business operations very well. To gain more insights and prevent biased judgments, we can use TOPSIS-DEA, which integrates two different types of DEA (i.e., BPF-DEA and WPF-DEA). The analyzed results are then inserted into PTWSVM to construct the model for performance forecasting. The result shows that the linguistic cues can be viewed as complements to numerical information by providing timely messages with any hysteresis and without a long time delay. As a lack of interpretability is one of the critical challenges of PTWSVM, this study performs FSA to open up the black-box of PTWSVM and represents the inherent knowledge in human-readable formats. Our study provides direct support for accounting regulators in Taiwan who have recently required a new section on risk factors be added into corporate annual reports so as to eliminate information asymmetry as well as maintain capital market stability.
Future works can consider several research directions as follows.

• Advanced topic modeling algorithm: This architecture can be extended to even a more sophisticated framework, such as Hierarchical Latent Dirichlet Allocation, in order to analyze the capability of its information extraction. • Advanced polarity identification method: This study can be adapted to a regression-based method instead of a binary classification method to determine the relative impact of each word and to infer the document score. This is because the regression-based method can take advantage of the sign and magnitude of messages. We also can adopt different statistical-based methods (such as LASSO) to infer the polarity of each word so as to reach a more conclusive outcome. • Advanced performance evaluation framework: The method adopted in handling the performance evaluation task is a black-box approach (i.e., TOPSIS-DEA). It can be opened up further to a network-based or dynamic-based DEA method so as to gain more insights.

paragraphs: Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) is an unsupervised technique for expressing a collection of documents according to their thematic content. It can categorize a document as a multinomial distribution over topics and the topics as a multinomial distribution over words. Via this approach, we can identify essential topics and labels and analyze the difference and relative essence among them. A topic ( z ) is a unique distribution over a vocabulary of words that managers use to illustrate their firm's potentials, risk factors, and competitive advantages that are encountered. Annual reports issued by publicly listed corporates that contain C words are referred to as "document" a = (a 1 , … a C ) , where a c is the c th word in the reports. Next, B optimistic or pessimistic reports of each corporate that constitute a dataset can be viewed as a "corpus" D = a 1 , … , a B , where a b is the m th reports in the corpus. Here, K denotes the number of topics across the corpus. Figure 4 dis- plays the graphical model illustration of LDA.

The observable and non-observable (latent) variables are represented as shadow circle (a) and non-shadow circles, respectively. The boxes are "plates" that express the replicates. The outer plate and the inner plate represent the annual report and the repeated choice of latent topics and words within the report, respectively. The symbol denotes a report-topic distribution that is determined by a symmetric Dirichlet prior , while expresses a topic-word distribution that is decided by a symmetric Dirichlet prior . Based on the prior notations, the generative process of LDA for each report in a corpus D can be expressed as follows (Blei et al. 2003).
• Select a topic z c ∼ Multinomial( ).
• Select a word a c from p(a c | | z c, ) , a multinomial probability grounded on topic z c .
We denote C as the length of the annual report, and expresses a k-dimensional Dir- ichlet random variable that poses a probability density function.

where and Γ(x) represent a k-vector and Gamma function, respectively. Here, depicts a k-dimensional Dirichlet random variable embedded into a determined topic. The Dirichlet distribution is a mixture of multinomial distribution. By joint utilization of and the summarization z , we obtain the likelihood of annual report. ϕ β θ To gain much deeper insights and unbiased outcomes, we implement TOPSIS (one of the MADM approaches) to integrate the results derived from WPF-DEA and BPF-DEA. The fundamental idea is that the best DMU must have the shortest distance from the outcome of BPF-DEA and the longest distance from the outcome of WPF-DEA. Thus, the relative distance (RC) measure is considered.
For more detailed illustrations of TOPSIS-DEA, one can refer to Wang and Luo (2006), Liu andChen (2009), andChen (2012).

paragraphs: Support vector machine (SVM), introduced by Cortes and Vapnik (1995), relies on the structural risk minimization (SRM) principle of maximum margin, dual theory, and kernel trick derived from the concept of statistical learning (Maldonado et al. 2017). While it strongly admired for handling forecasting tasks, it still comes with some challenges, such as the establishment of its two parallel decision surfaces being a complicated quadratic programming problem (QPP) that takes considerable computational efforts to solve (López and Maldonado 2017). Apart from SVM, twin support vector machine (TWSVM) seeks two non-parallel decision surfaces such that each surface is closest to one of the two class labels and as far as possible from the other. The difference between SVM and TWSVM is that TWSVM solves two reduced QPPs instead of a large one in SVM. From the view of computational efficiency, TWSVM's calculation speed is around four times faster and also poses comparative generalization ability of SVM (Jayadeva et al. 2007). To enhance the performance of TWSVM, Chen et al. (2011) introduced the projection twin support vector machine (PTWSVM), which seeks two projection directions so as to let the data-points of one class label be closest to its class label while at the same time the data-points of a dissimilar class label are separated as far as possible. A brief illustration of PTWSVM is expressed as follows. x (i) j ∈ R are the j th input belonging to class label i , and b = b 1 + b 2 , y j ∈ (±1) denotes the corresponding outcomes. We can further organize the b 1 inputs of class label 1 by matrix G ∈ R b 1 ×n and the b 2 inputs of class label 2 by matrix H ∈ R b 2 ×n .

The aim of PTWSVM is to seek a projection axis for each class label, such that the within-class variance of the projected data-points of its own class label is minimized while at the same time the projected data-points of the other class scatter away to a certain extent (Shao et al. 2013). sections.preview_head: Support vector machine (SVM), introduced by Cortes and Vapnik (1995), relies on the structural risk minimization (SRM) principle of maximum margin, dual theory, and kernel trick derived from the concept of statistical learning (Maldonado et al. 2017). While it strongly admired for handling forecasting tasks, it still comes with some challenges, such as the establishment of its two parallel decision surfaces being a complicated quadratic programming problem (QPP) that takes considerable computational efforts to solve (López and Maldonado 2017). Apart from SVM, twin support vector machine (TWSVM) seeks two non-parallel decision surfaces such that each surface is closest to one of the two class labels and as far as possible from the other.
